text: Welcome to this video of our initial experience using Artessential VATS wristed instruments. The latest version of these is now shorter with an ideal length for thoracic surgery and has a locking mechanism for port entry. These robotic-like instruments are powered by the surgeon rather than electronics and apply a fully articulating wristed motion allowing far greater flexibility than traditional port instruments. We have used these instruments in six thoracic cases and would like to show you some examples.

The surgeon performs atesiolysis using a bipolar Maryland dissector through a subxiphoid port, an anterior camera port, and two anterior utility ports. The articulating wristed motion of the instrument allows precise angling for dissection, while the bipolar energy device coagulates small vessels with minimal energy displacement.

The surgeon dissects around the anterior hilum using wristed instruments, including a bipolar fenestrated forceps for blunt dissection. The Maryland dissector is controlled to follow the ideal tissue planes, demonstrating precise manipulation similar to robotic procedures.

The surgeon uses bipolar fenestrated forceps to dissect around the inferior pulmonary vein, preparing the path for stapling. A full range of wristed instruments, including a bipolar Maryland dissector and hook diathermy, is visible.

The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector to carefully dissect the pleura over the anterior hilum, identifying the superior pulmonary vein and pulmonary artery. Fenestrated forceps are employed to encircle the superior pulmonary vein, utilizing their articulated movement to achieve a complete ninety-degree angle for precise dissection around large blood vessels. The surgeon staples the vein using a subxiphoid port approach. Lymph nodes located behind the superior pulmonary vein are grasped with fenestrated forceps and dissected out using a bipolar Maryland dissector, demonstrating the ability to maneuver at various angles without damaging the nodes.

The surgeon dissects around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch using alternating Maryland dissector and fenestrated forceps, creating a clear path for stapling. The instruments' angulation aids in precise dissection, and tactile feedback allows careful manipulation around the blood vessel. The Cavidian Tristapler with the Signia gun is introduced through the subxiphoid port to fire staples.

The surgeon begins dissecting at the junction of the pericardium and fat, following the phrenic nerve upwards using a bipolar Maryland dissector and fenestrated forceps for retraction and dissection. The instruments provide excellent angles and subtle wrist movements for precise articulation. The innominate vein, rhema vein, and superior vena cava (SVC) are clearly visible as the surgeon removes fat between the innominate and rhema veins. The tumor is visible above the instruments, with the phrenic nerve lying over the SVC, demonstrating the effectiveness of the wristed instruments in navigating tissue planes.

To come back to the training aspects that is required for these instruments, training videos are also available to practice various tasks. It does take some time getting used to the combination of VATS instruments and robotic instrument moves, so we do recommend you practice sufficiently before using these on a patient. The major challenge is that these instruments work in a half VATS, half robotic style, as they pivot on the ports in a VATS way, but the wrist moves exactly like using robotics instruments. So overall it is our opinion that there are some major advantages associated with using these instruments in your VATS practice which include safety and cost. We think these have an incredible potential for thoracic surgery and have added considerably to our standard practice. We do think that there are also other possible indications where these instruments might become useful such as in mitral valve surgery and we can't wait to hear about that from other colleagues.
